questions.text: Competitive inhibitors and real substrate often have similar?
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Because of structural similarity the competitive inhibitors may Bind to the active site but are not able to activate the catalytic sites.

questions.text: Nucleotides are building blocks of nucleic acids. Each nucleotide is a composite molecule formed by
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: (a) : Each nucleotide consists of three distinct units ­ a phosphate group derived from phosphoric acid, a pentose sugar, and a ring-shaped nitrogenous base. Nucleoside + Phosphoric acid= Nucleotide + H 2 O

questions.text: The type of inhibition in which inhibitor has no structural similarity to substrate and combines with enzyme at other than the active site is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In such inhibition the inhibitor forms covalent bonds with the active site of enzyme or physically block the sote completely. The active site is destroyed and remains no more available to substrate.

This option is incorrect. In such inhibition the inhibitor resembles substrate structurally thereby competing with it for the active site. This is inhibition is overcome by increasing substrate concentration.

This option is correct. The inhibitor does not resemble with substrate structurally hence it doesnot compete with it and its effect can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration thereby restoring enzymatic activity. That is why this reversibl

This option is incorrect. Reversible inhibition involves reversible inhibitors which form weak linkages with the enzyme. Increasing the substrate concentration can neutralize the effect of the inhibitor partly or completely. It is further of two types: 1. Competitive inhibition, 2. Non-competitive inhibition.

questions.explanation: Non-competitive reversible inhibitor block allosteric site of enzyme, while competitive inhibitors have structural similarity with substrate, and they bind active site of enzyme.

questions.text: The view that active site of an enzyme is flexible and when a substrate combines with it, cause changes in enzyme structure is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. According to lock and key model, the active site is a rigid site which is not modified when substrate binds to it.

This option is correct. According to the induced fit model, the active site of an enzyme is not rigid. As the substrate binds to the enzyme, its active site is modified accordingly to facilitate its binding.
This option is incorrect. Sliding filament model provides information about the mechanism by which muscle contracts.
This option is incorrect. This model explains that every enzyme is specific with regard to its substrate and reacts only with its specific substrate.

questions.explanation: According to the lock and key model, the active site of an enzyme is a rigid structure. The sliding filament model explains muscle movement, while the specificity model means that every enzyme reacts with a specific substrate.

questions.text: The optimum pH value for the working of pancreatic lipase is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. 4.50 is the optimum pH of sucrase.
This option is incorrect. 7.60 is the optimum pH of catalase.
This option is incorrect. 2.00 is the optimum pH of pepsin.
This option is correct. Optimum pH pf pancreatic lipase is 9.00.

questions.explanation: Optimum pH values for the catalytic activity of pepsin, sucrose, catalase and pancreatic lipase are 2.00, 4.50, 7.00 and 9.00, respectively.

questions.text: A cofactor tightly bound to the enzyme on the permanent basis is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Activator is basically a detachable co-factor which is non-protein part affiliated with the enzyme. These are inorganic ions. This option is incorrect.
Some enzymes are purely proteins, others may have non-protein part associated with it. If the non-protein part is attached loosely with enzyme, it is known as coenzyme. This option is also incorrect.
Enzymes which are not completely proteins have non-protein part affiliated with them. If a non-protein part is bonded covalently, it is known as a prosthetic group. This option is correct.
This option is incorrect. Apoenzyme is an enzyme with its prosthetic group or coenzyme removed.

questions.explanation: The detachable cofactor is known as an activator if it is an inorganic ion. If the non-protein part is covalently bonded, it is known as a prosthetic group. If it is loosely attached to the protein part, it is known as coenzyme. An enzyme with its cofactor removed is called apoenzyme.

questions.text: Monosaccharides are major components of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA, ATP, Ribulose bisphosphate and Cysteine
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Cytosine is a nitrogenous bases which doesnot have any monosaccharide in it. While ATP, DNA and ribulose bisphosphate do have it.
This option is incorrect. Insulin is a hormone made of protein only and do not have any monosaccharide in it. While ATP, and NAD do have it.

options.text: DNA, NADP, ATP and Ribulose bisphosphate
options.explanation: This option is correct. All of these compounds have monosaccharides as their component and part of structure. Like DNA has deoxyribose.
This option is incorrect. Myosin is a protein only and do not have any monosaccharide in it. While ATP and RNA do have it.

questions.explanation: Ribose is a five carbon sugar and a monosaccharide. It is a major component of nucleosides/ nucleotides, dinucleotides, nucleic acid and RuBP (carbon dioxide acceptor in dark reaction of photosynthesis).

questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of a competitive inhibitor?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Glucose is a simple sugar that is an important source of energy for the body. It is not a competitive inhibitor.

This option is incorrect. Fumarate is a molecule involved in the citric acid cycle, which is a series of enzymatic reactions that occurs in the mitochondria of cells. It is not a competitive inhibitor.
This option is incorrect. Succinic acid, also known as succinate, is another molecule involved in the citric acid cycle. It is not a competitive inhibitor.
This option is correct. Malonate is a three-carbon dicarboxylic acid. It is well known as a competitive inhibitor of succinate dehydrogenase.

questions.explanation: Malonate has structural similarity with succinic acid and it binds competitively with the active site of succinic acid dehydrogenase enzyme and blocks its activity.

questions.text: A powerful digesting enzyme that is capable of destroying cell's internal structure is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a lipid digesting enzyme.
It is a DNA digesting enzyme.
It is a phospholipid digesting enzyme. Cell membrane is composed of phospholipid bilayer, so this enzy
It is a protein digesting enzyme. Cell's internal environment is mainly made of proteins. So for its digestion, a protein digesting enzyme is required which is pepsin.

questions.explanation: Since cell's internal ernvironment is mainly made of proteins. So for its digestion, a protein digesting enzyme is required which is pepsin.

questions.text: The transition state structure of the substrate formed during an enzymatic reaction is:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The transition state is the formation of the unstable intermediate structural state. During this, substrate bonds are broken and new bonds are established that transform the substrate molecules into products. This state is transient and highly unstable.

questions.text: Three of the following statements about enzymes are correct and one is wrong. Which one is wrong?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Enzymes require optimum pH for maximal activity
Enzymes are denatured at high temperatures but in certain exceptional organisms, they are effective even at temperatures such as 80° and­ 90°C
Most enzymes are proteins but some are lipids

questions.explanation: Enzymes are mostly proteins but some are RNA (ribozymes). No lipid which works as an enzyme is known.

questions.text: Modern detergents contain enzyme preparations of:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Modern detergents contain enzyme preparation of alkaline proteases which are called alkaliphiles. These help in removing protein stains.

questions.text: The lower the activation energy, the _ the reaction will be.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lowering the activation energy will increase the rate of reaction.
Lowering the activation energy will increase the rate of reaction to maximum rate.
Lowering the activation energy will not decrease the rate of reaction.
Lowering the activation energy will increase the rate of reaction.

questions.explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts, which speed up rate of chemical reaction by lowering energy of activation.

questions.text: _suggested that each enzyme had a particular shape into which the substrate fit exactly.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: He introduced the "induced fit" model, where enzymes can change their shape slightly to accommodate the substrate.
Known for his work in biological classification, not directly related to enzyme-substrate interactions.

Emil Fischer proposed the "lock and key" model, which suggests that enzymes and substrates have specific complementary shapes that allow them to fit together like a lock and key. This model implies that the enzyme's active site is already in the appropriate shape for the substrate to bind, without requiring significant conformational changes.
Developed the Michaelis-Menten equation, a fundamental concept in enzyme kinetics. Not related to proposing the concept of specific enzyme-substrate shapes.

questions.explanation: Emil Fischer proposed the "lock and key" model, which suggests that enzymes and substrates have specific complementary shapes that allow them to fit together like a lock and key. This model implies that the enzyme's active site is already in the appropriate shape for the substrate to bind, without requiring significant conformational changes.

questions.text: If the concentration of substrate molecule is higher than the enzymes then rate of reaction would be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If the concentration of substrate molecules is higher than the concentration of enzymes, then the rate of reaction would be limited by the amount of enzyme present. In this case, adding more substrate would not increase the rate of reaction, as there would not be enough enzyme to catalyze the reaction. However, if the concentration of enzyme is higher than the concentration of substrate, then increasing the concentration of substrate would increase the rate of reaction until the enzyme becomes saturated.

Ionic bonds are sensitive to pH wheras hydrogen bonds are sensitive to temperature.

questions.text: This figure represents_inhibitor.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A non-competitive inhibitor is a type of enzyme inhibitor that binds to an enzyme at a location other than the active site, causing a conformational change in the enzyme's structure. Unlike competitive inhibitors that directly compete with the substrate for binding at the active site, non-competitive inhibitors do not compete for the same binding site as the substrate.

An irreversible inhibitor is a type of enzyme inhibitor that covalently binds to the enzyme, resulting in permanent and long-lasting inactivation of the enzyme's catalytic activity. Unlike reversible inhibitors, which can bind and dissociate from the enzyme, irreversible inhibitors form a strong, covalent bond with the enzyme's active site or another critical site, making the inhibition non-reversible.
In competitive inhibition, the inhibitor attaches with the active site of the enzyme and prevents the attachment of the substrate.

An "isosteric inhibitor" refers to a type of enzyme inhibitor that shares a similar or nearly identical shape and size with the natural substrate of the enzyme. This similarity allows the isosteric inhibitor to bind to the enzyme's active site and compete with the natural substrate for binding.

questions.explanation: Non-competitive inhibitor binds allosteric site of enzyme rather than active site.

questions.text: A student of chemical engineering mistakenly engulfed the toxic compound "A" which was a potent inhibitor of certain enzyme. He was immediately brought to hospital where the doctor injected intravenously substrate "B" to minimize the toxic effect of compound A. His life was saved from serious damages. The treatment method shows that compound A was a_ inhibitors.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Noncompetitive inhibitors are substances that bind to the enzyme at a site other than the active site, known as the allosteric site. This binding causes a conformational change in the enzyme, which reduces its activity regardless of the substrate concentration. Noncompetitive inhibition is not overcome by increasing the substrate concentration because the inhibitor and the substrate do not compete for the same binding site.

Irreversible inhibitors covalently bind to the enzyme and permanently inactivate it. The fact that the toxic effects of compound A were minimized by injecting substrate B indicates that compound A's effects were not irreversible.

Competitive inhibitors are substances that compete with the substrate for binding to the active site of the enzyme. They have a similar chemical structure to the substrate, allowing them to bind to the active site and prevent the substrate from binding. The binding of a competitive inhibitor is reversible, meaning it can be overcome by increasing the concentration of the substrate.In the given scenario, the doctor injected substrate B intravenously to minimize the toxic effects of compound A. By administering substrate B, the doctor increased the concentration of the substrate, which can outcompete compound A for binding to the active site of the enzyme. This helps to reduce the inhibitory effect of compound A and restore the enzyme's activity enzyme.
This option is incorrect since compound A's behavior aligns with the characteristics of a competitive reversible inhibitor, as described above.

questions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors are substances that compete with the substrate for binding to the active site of the enzyme. They have a similar chemical structure to the substrate, allowing them to bind to the active site and prevent the substrate from binding. The binding of a competitive inhibitor is reversible, meaning it can be overcome by increasing the concentration of the substrate.In the given scenario, the doctor injected substrate B intravenously to minimize the toxic effects of compound A. By administering substrate B, the doctor increased the concentration of the substrate, which can outcompete compound A for binding to the active site of the enzyme. This helps to reduce the inhibitory effect of compound A and restore the enzyme's activity enzyme.

questions.text: Conversion of glucose 6-phosphate into fructose 6-phosphate is done by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is Incorrect. conversion of fructose 6-phosphate from glucose 6-phosphate is a glycolytic pathway reaction and is catalyzed by phosphohexose isomerase. Glucose and fructose are isomers to each other so an enzyme that catalyzes this reaction is classified as Isomerase.

This is correct, conversion of fructose 6-phosphate from glucose 6-phosphate is a glycolytic pathway reaction and is catalyzed by phosphohexose isomerase. Glucose and fructose are isomers to each other so an enzyme that catalyzes this reaction is classified as Isomerase.

This is Incorrect. conversion of fructose 6-phosphate from glucose 6-phosphate is a glycolytic pathway reaction and is catalyzed by phosphohexose isomerase. Glucose and fructose are isomers to each other so an enzyme that catalyzes this reaction is classified as Isomerase.

This is Incorrect. conversion of fructose 6-phosphate from glucose 6-phosphate is a glycolytic pathway reaction and is catalyzed by phosphohexose isomerase. Glucose and fructose are isomers to each other so an enzyme that catalyzes this reaction is classified as Isomerase.

questions.explanation: conversion of fructose 6-phosphate from glucose 6-phosphate is a glycolytic pathway reaction and is catalyzed by phosphohexose isomerase. Glucose and fructose are isomers to each other so an enzyme that catalyzes this reaction is classified as Isomerase.
